coredata.dc:title: Synthesis and characterization of high-performance ZnO/graphene quantum dot composites for photocatalytic degradation of metronidazole
coredata.prism:publicationName: Journal of the Taiwan Institute of Chemical Engineers
coredata.prism:copyright: © 2021 Taiwan Institute of Chemical Engineers. Published by Elsevier B.V. All rights reserved.
coredata.prism:publisher: Taiwan Institute of Chemical Engineers. Published by Elsevier B.V.
coredata.dc:creator: Hsieh, Meng-Lin
Juang, Ruey-Shin
Gandomi, Yasser Ashraf
Fu, Chun-Chieh
Hsieh, Chien-Te
Liu, Wei-Ren
coredata.dc:description:
Background Industrial and medical wastewater treatment has been the focus of significant research and development in recent years. Especially untreated antibiotics commonly found within the wastewater generated by the hospitals or/and manufacturers have caused serious environmental concerns.
Methods In this work, we proposed a heterogeneous graphene quantum dot (GQD)/ZnO composite as a photocatalyst for degrading antibiotics by using co-precipitation and thermal carbonization processes.
Significant findings The introduction of N-GQDs efficiently facilitates the photocatalytic activity and reaction rate. Indeed, the N-functionalized GQDs, including pyrrolic/pyridinic N and graphitic N, can accelerate the charge transfer rate in the heterostructures. Optimizing the composition of GQD/ZnO catalyst, ultra-high removal ratio (∼100%) and significantly improved rate constant (i.e., 1.74 times higher compared to pristine ZnO) was achieved. In the binary catalyst, the presence of N-GQDs enhances the absorption in visible-light region and increases the photo-induced charge carrier, resulting in an elevated photocatalytic activity. This finding reveals that the GQD/ZnO composite structure demonstrated in this work can be employed as an in-expensive photocatalyst for degrading metronidazole (MNZ) in liquid phase under the UV-light irradiation.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Graphene quantum dots
Zinc oxides
Photocatalysts
Metronidazole
Nitrogen doping

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Photocatalytic degradation of MNZ under UV irradiation

xocs:cp-notices.xocs:cp-notice: © 2021 Taiwan Institute of Chemical Engineers. Published by Elsevier B.V. All rights reserved.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Schematic diagrams of (a) synthesis flowchart and (b)structure of GQD/ZnO composites.

(a) PL excitation and PL emission spectra of N-GQD suspensions (Inset is the pictures of N-GQD suspension illuminated by sunlight and UV irradiation) and (b) PL emission spectra as a function of excitation sources with different wavelengths.
UV-vis absorption spectra of ZnO, N-GQD, and GQD/ZnO composite.
XRD patterns of ZnO, N-GQD and GQD/ZnO samples.
TGA curves of ZnO and GQD/ZnO sample with a heating rate of 5 °C/min under air atmosphere.
XPS spectra of GQD/ZnO composite: (a) C 1s, (b) N 1s, (c) O 1s, and (d) Zn 2p.
Adsorption-desorption isotherms and pore size distributions of (a, b) ZnO and (c, d) GQD/ZnO samples.

HR-TEM micrographs of (a) ZnO, (c, d) N-GQD, and (e, f) GQD/ZnO samples. For clarification, SAD patterns of (b) ZnO was given, and the SAD patterns of the other two samples were inserted into the inset of Fig. 6(c) and (e).

Photocatalytic degradation of MNZ by GQD/ZnO composite under UV irradiation: (a) reaction kinetic curves and (b) plots of –ln (C/C0) vs. time for degradation of MNZ. The inset of (a) shows the photograph of GQD/ZnO composite.
Photocatalytic degradation of MNZ by different photocatalysts under UV irradiation: (a) reaction kinetic curves and (b) plots of –ln (C/C0) vs. time for degradation of MNZ.

(a) Cyclic voltammetry profiles of pristine ZnO and GQD/ZnO composite electrodes and (b) the proposed photo-induced electron dynamic pathway of GQD/ZnO composite under sunlight illumination, where CB and VB represent conduction band and valence band, respectively.
Elemental analyses of GQD/ZnO, N-GQD and ZnO samples.
Photocatalytic kinetic parameters of GQD/ZnO samples under UV irradiation.

head.ce:title: Synthesis and characterization of high-performance ZnO/graphene quantum dot composites for photocatalytic degradation of metronidazole

ce:correspondence.ce:text: Corresponding author at: Department of Chemical Engineering and Materials Science, Yuan Ze University, Taoyuan 32003, Taiwan.

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Department of Chemical Engineering and Materials Science

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Industrial and medical wastewater treatment has been the focus of significant research and development in recent years. Especially untreated antibiotics commonly found within the wastewater generated by the hospitals or/and manufacturers have caused serious environmental concerns.
In this work, we proposed a heterogeneous graphene quantum dot (GQD)/ZnO composite as a photocatalyst for degrading antibiotics by using co-precipitation and thermal carbonization processes.

The introduction of N-GQDs efficiently facilitates the photocatalytic activity and reaction rate. Indeed, the N-functionalized GQDs, including pyrrolic/pyridinic N and graphitic N, can accelerate the charge transfer rate in the heterostructures. Optimizing the composition of GQD/ZnO catalyst, ultra-high removal ratio (∼100%) and significantly improved rate constant (i.e., 1.74 times higher compared to pristine ZnO) was achieved. In the binary catalyst, the presence of N-GQDs enhances the absorption in visible-light region and increases the photo-induced charge carrier, resulting in an elevated photocatalytic activity. This finding reveals that the GQD/ZnO composite structure demonstrated in this work can be employed as an in-expensive photocatalyst for degrading metronidazole (MNZ) in liquid phase under the UV-light irradiation.

ce:section.ce:para: Industrial and medical wastewater treatment has been the focus of significant research and development in recent years. Specifically, untreated antibiotics frequently found within the wastewater generated by the hospitals or/and manufacturers have caused serious environmental concerns [1–3]. Commonly used approach for treating the antibiotic wastewater is the chemical oxidation method. Although this method is very effective in degrading a variety of organic pollutants; needing excessive amount of oxidants during the reaction processing has significantly limited its widespread utilization. In addition, the oxidants can potentially react with each other and generate various chemical sludges or even solid wastes. To address this critical issue, several alternative technologies have been developed to treat the antibiotic wastewater such as flotation process [4], adsorption technique [5], air flotation methodology [6], and the Fenton method [7–9]. Among these systems, photocatalytic approach is very promising with very little pollution and extremely low environmental impact. Photocatalysts have been successfully employed in different types of oxidation reactions to degrade and remove residual drugs in water. When a photocatalyst is irradiated under an appropriate light with sufficient excitation energy, the electrons will jump from the valence to the conduction band, generating electron-hole pairs. The electron-hole pairs participate in the redox reaction where the photo-induced species act as an oxidizing or reducing agent to degrade the substances adsorbed on the surface of the photocatalyst [10]. For instance, one water molecule adsorbed on the catalyst's surface can be photo-induced to produce hydroxyl radicals that react with organic compounds to remove the pollutants [11].

Photocatalysts can be categorized as extrinsic semiconductor materials; thus, not having adequate free electrons, generating electric current using photocatalysts is relatively difficult. However, the conductivity of photocatalysts can be altered via metal doping or coating a conductive film. Because of its sensitivity to light, the photocatalysts can also be considered as “photo-semiconductors” [12]. If the photon energy is greater than the energy gap of the semiconductor, its internal electrons will be excited to an elevated state, leaving a hole within the structure. The resulting electron-hole generated by the light usually initiates a redox reaction to adsorb water or organic matter on the surface of the semiconductor [13,14]. Zinc oxide (ZnO) and titanium dioxide (TiO2) are both optical semiconductors, in which ZnO has a wider energy gap than TiO2 at ambient temperature (i.e., energy gap: 3.37 eV, binding energy: 60 meV); and it also demonstrates higher ultraviolet/near ultraviolet light absorption capability [15–17,37,39–53]. Like TiO2, ZnO is an inexpensive photocatalyst with excellent anti-fouling, antibacterial, and photocatalytic properties [18–21]. The photocatalytic mechanism of the ZnO is initiated when the pollutants are adsorbed on the surface. Followingly, the ZnO induces oxidation-reduction reaction in the adsorption phase and simultaneously degrades the pollutants, generating electron-hole pairs through the photo-induced process, and eventually produces hydroxyl radicals to deteriorate the pollutants on the surface of zinc oxide [22,23]. Although ZnO is very promising, its performance is still limited by the rapid recombination of electron-hole pairs, resulting in low photocatalytic efficiency. In addition, due to its large energy gap, the photocatalytic conversion on ZnO catalysts is hugely affected by its light absorption capacity. Therefore, significant prior efforts have been dedicated for improving the photocatalytic activity of ZnO [24]. For instance, Zhang et al. found that reduced crystallinity and increased defects within ZnO nanowires are advantageous to its photocatalytic efficiency [25]. The improved performance mainly originated from the fact that the electron-hole pairs generated by light illumination can be captured through surface defects. Accordingly, reducing the recombination rate of electron-hole pairs is critical to enhance its separation efficiency and hydroxyl radical generation [25]. Liu et al. used the sol-gel method to prepare graphene-loaded ZnO composite [26]. The enhanced catalytic activity was attributed to the adsorption capability of organic pollutants due to the π-π conjugate interaction between the pollutant molecules and the sp2 graphene surface [26]. They also reported on the importance of an optimal graphene content within the composites to enhance the adsorption capacity of contaminant molecules. With an excessive graphene content, the degradation efficiency decreased due to the shading effect and surface scattering [27].

To prepare highly efficient photocatalysts, in this work, we have synthesized graphene quantum dot (GQD)/ZnO catalytic composites. The GQD/ZnO composite photocatalyst generates numerous electron-hole pairs under the UV-light, forming hydroxyl and super oxygen radicals in the presence of water and air. The resulting radicals degrade the antibiotic pollutants through the redox reactions and decompose them into less toxic or non-toxic compounds. The environmentally friendly photocatalysis method demonstrated in this study can be employed for preparing efficient composite photocatalysts capable of degrading metronidazole (MNZ) in liquid phase (primarily for the antibiotic wastewater treatment).

The schematic illustration of GQD/ZnO composites’ synthesis procedure has been provided in Fig. 1 (a). First, 1.5 g of citric acid (99.5%, Sigma Aldrich) was mixed with 0.5 g of urea (99%, Sigma Aldrich) and the resulting mixture was placed into an alumina crucible while adding 1 mL of deionized (DI) water to prepare a homogeneous slurry. The slurry was then put in a furnace and was thermally treated in air (250 °C for 2.5 h). The carbonization process enabled the formation of N-doped GQD samples. Afterward, the as-prepared GQD samples were collected and grounded using a three-dimensional ball-milling machine. Next, different quantities of GQD powders (X = 0.3, 0.5, 1, 2, and 3 g) were mixed with 50 mL of DI water; oscillated in an ultrasonic oscillator for 0.5 h; and centrifuged at a high rotational speed (10,000 rpm) for 10 min to remove the remaining carbon chars from the GQD suspensions. To prepare GQD/ZnO composites, 0.01 mole of zinc nitrate (99%, Showa) was added to 200 mL of DI water and stirred for 0.5 h. Subsequently, the Zn-containing solution was uniformly mixed with GQD suspension. After stirring, 1 g of polyvinylpyrrolidone (PVP, 99.99%, PVPK30, Acros Organics) was placed into the solution. Finally, one co-precipitation method was applied for depositing ZnO nanoparticles in the presence of GQDs, forming GQD/ZnO composites. Here we added 1.6 g of sodium hydroxide into the solution and then stirred for 3 h to obtain the final GQDs/ZnO-X composites.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Photocatalytic degradation of MNZ under UV irradiation
ce:section.ce:para: First, 500 ppm of MNZ (99.99%, Sigma Aldrich) solution was prepared via dissolving 1 g of MNZ into a two-liter flask of DI water. Then, additional configurations of the MNZ solution were prepared via diluting the initial composition to various concentrations (i.e., 10, 50, 100, and 200 ppm). The resulting mixtures were utilized for conducting calibration curve analysis. Herein we selected 100 ppm for the adsorption kinetic measurements at room temperature (pH value is 6.5), where 0.1 g of GQD/ZnO-X sample was initially dispersed in MNZ aqueous solution (100 mL). Every 5 min, the MNZ concentration was measured, and the entire experiment was carried out for ∼1 h. Analyzing the variation in MNZ concentration overtime, since almost no concentration change was observed after ∼15 min, the adsorption equilibrium was obtained.

Finally, 0.1 g of GQD/ZnO-X composite was added into 100 mL of 100 ppm MNZ solution for the photocatalytic degradation experiments. After the adsorption saturation was attained in dark, as soon as the UV lamp was turned on, the photocatalytic reaction was initiated. The MNZ concentration was then sampled at 0, 60, 90, 120, 150, and 180 min during the photocatalysis process. High-performance liquid chromatography (HPLC, PU2089 plus, Jasco) was employed to analyze the MNZ concentration. Herein the extraction solution composed of 0.05 M KH2PO4/methanol (in 95:5 in v/v, pH = 4 ± 0.01), with the volume of ∼20 μL for each HPLC analysis at 365 nm UV irradiation (power source: 100 W).

The GQD/ZnO composites were characterized by powder X-ray diffraction (XRD, Bruker D8 Advance Eco) with Cu‒Kα radiation (λ = 1.5418 Å). The surface morphology and structure of the GQD/ZnO composites were analyzed using field-emission scanning electron microscope (FE-SEM, JEOL, JSM-7600F) and energy dispersive X-ray spectroscopy (EDS). High-resolution transmission electron microscopy (HR-TEM, JEOL, JEM-2100) was also employed to record the micrographs of GQD/ZnO composites with an acceleration voltage of 200 kV. Thermogravimetric analyzer (TGA, DuPont, TA Q50) was used to analyze the carbon content of GQDs/ZnO composites at a heating rate of 10 °C min−1 within a temperature range of 30–800 °C under the air atmosphere. The photoluminescence (PL) excitation and emission spectra of as-prepared samples were analyzed using the Horiba Jobin-Yvon FluoroMax4 spectrometer with a 450 W Xe light source. Absorption spectra of GQD/ZnO suspensions were taken by a UV-Vis spectrophotometer (U-2900, Hitachi). Raman spectra were obtained via a micro-Raman spectroscopy system with a laser frequency of 532 nm as the excitation source. X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS, K-Alpha) was adopted to measure the chemical composition and surface functionalities of GQD/ZnO samples. Porosity measurements (Tristar 3000) were conducted to measure the Brunauer-Emmett-Teller (BET) surface area and Barrett-Joyner-Halenda (BJH) mesopore volume through N2 physisorption at -196 °C.

Fig. 1(b) shows the schematic diagram of GQD/ZnO composite photocatalysts, where the N-functionalized GQDs are decorated on the ZnO surface. In the structure of bi-functional GQD/ZnO photocatalyst, ZnO acts as an efficient photo-induced active site for degrading MNZ molecules, whereas GQDs provide the essential adsorption sites for the MNZ species and serve as the charge carrier for modulating the optical band gap of the semiconductor (i.e., ZnO crystals). To enhance the photocatalytic activity, it is necessary to optimize the ZnO to GQD ratio since excessive amount of GQDs probably hinders the light illumination, whereas insufficient loading of GQDs will have minor influence on the optical properties of the composite photocatalysts.

Prior to analyzing the photocatalytic performance, PL excitation and emission spectra of the GQD suspensions were recorded, as shown in Fig. 2(a). According to Fig. 2(a), the GQD suspension displays an optimal excitation wavelength at ca. 440 nm, while the PL emission takes place at the maximal wavelength of ∼560 nm. The insets of Fig. 2(a) include the photographs of GQD suspension under the sunlight and 365 nm excitation. We observe that the color of GQD suspension under the UV excitation is blue-greenish. Fig. 2(b) exhibits the PL spectra of GQD suspension under different excitation wavelengths, ranging from 300 to 500 nm. It can be seen from Fig. 2(b) that as-synthesized GQD samples exhibit a strong excitation-dependent attribute. It is important to note that since GQDs contain numerous surface defects upon nitrogen doping, these defects can potentially create different kinds of surface photo-induced states. In addition, certain functional groups, such as C‒OH, C=C, C=O, or C=N, on the surface of N-doped GQDs can form multiple energy levels between the π-π* gap along with generating corresponding electronic transitional levels within the initial energy gap. Accordingly, some specific energy levels dominate the PL emission at the excitation wavelength; that is, as the excitation wavelength varies, the N-doped GQD suspension continues to illuminate different PL emissions. The corresponding energy levels, majorly due to N-doping, will alter the light emission, which can be inferred through the excitation-dependent characteristics of N-GQD suspensions [28–30].

Fig. 3 shows UV-Vis absorption spectra of ZnO, N-GQD, and GQD/ZnO suspensions. The absorption spectrum of N-GQD sample displays two main bands located at ca. 260 and ca. 350 nm, which is usually attributed to the contribution of π-π* transition of C=C bond (∼260 nm) and n-π* transition of C=O and C=N bonds (∼350 nm), respectively [31]. Thus, the light source with the wavelengths of 292–450 nm can be excited to produce the PL emission of N-GQD suspension. The energy gap of N-GQD sample was estimated to be 2.52 eV by extrapolating of the absorption spectrum at ∼488 nm. The absorption band of ZnO mainly concentrates within UV region, indicating its absorption edge at the wavelength of ∼390 nm; whereas no absorption bands can be observed in the visible light region (> 400 nm). From the equation of band gap = 1240/λ, the energy gap of ZnO was estimated to be 3.18 eV, which is in good agreement with previously reported values [32,33]. In the heterostructure of GQD/ZnO composite, the absorption edge can be clearly expanded by adding N-GQDs within the range of its original absorption edge (∼390 nm) to ∼400 nm. Also, as depicted in Fig. 3, a partial absorption can be observed in the range of 400–600 nm. This observation confirms that implementing N-GQDs into ZnO matrix improves the absorption within the UV-light region and extends the absorption edge to the visible light domain. This trend is also consistent with the report of Min et al., where they added GQDs to TiO2 catalyst to widen the absorption band in the visible-light region [34]. This finding reveals that the energy gap of as-synthesized GQD/ZnO composite clearly shifts to ∼3.06 eV (i.e., 405 nm). As a result, the heterostructure including the ZnO and N-GQDs can be considered as a visible-light-derived photocatalyst.

Fig. 4 shows typical XRD patterns of pure (or pristine) ZnO, N-GQD and GQD/ZnO composite. For pure ZnO, the diffraction peaks located at 31.8°, 34.4°, 36.3°, 47.5°, 56.5°, 62.9°, 66.4°, 67.9°, and 69.2° can be assigned to the crystal planes (100), (002), (101), (102), (110), (103), (200), (112) and (201) in wurtzite ZnO lattices, respectively. All the diffraction peaks are in good agreement with the ZnO standard pattern reported in the ICSD #23-0511 (i.e., wurtzite structure). As for the N-GQD sample, the diffraction peak located at 26.8° can be contributed from a typical (002) crystal plane of a graphite-like material. The lattice interlayer spacing of (002) was determined to be 3.32 Å via applying the Bragg's law. As for the GQD/ZnO composite, the position of the characteristic peaks of ZnO did not shift after introducing N-GQDs. Both diffraction peaks of ZnO and N-GQD still appear in the XRD patterns of GQD/ZnO composite. Fig. S1 shows the Raman spectra of ZnO, N-GQD and GQD/ZnO composite. The peak at ca. 1580 cm−1 can be assigned to the G band of the sp2 bonding vibration of C=C (i.e., crystalline graphite). The peak at ca. 1334 cm−1 corresponds to the D band of the sp3 bonding vibration with C‒C as the major vibrational mode. For the N-GQD samples, a stronger G band is usually indicative of a graphene-like robust structure. On the contrary, higher D band is commonly observed with the sp3-dominant carbon microstructures (i.e., highly defective carbon morphology). Also, as illustrated in Fig. S1, the Raman spectrum of the N-GQD sample demonstrates higher D band intensity with an ID/IG ratio of 1.04. This reveals that N-GQD sample contains plenty of structural defects that have probably been formed during the thermal carbonization process. Compared to the ZnO Raman spectrum, the GQD/ZnO composite has an obvious D band signal at ∼1344 cm−1, indicating that the N-GQDs are successfully attached to the ZnO surface. Fig. 5 includes the TGA curves of pure ZnO and GQD/ZnO composite recorded with a heating rate of 5 °C/min under the air atmosphere. According to Fig. 5, a weight loss at ∼100‒150 °C can be observed primarily due to the vaporization of the solvent (i.e., water) adsorbed within the GQD/ZnO composite. Further increase in temperature results in burning off the N-GQDs from the GQD/ZnO composite, which usually occurs at about 250 °C and tends to generate CO2 and other volatiles. The weight content of N-GQDs in the GQD/ZnO composite can be estimated to be ca. 32.2 wt.%.

To explore the chemical composition of as-prepared GQD/ZnO composites, XPS was employed to quantify the C, N, O and Zn elements within the samples, as shown in Fig. 6 . According to C 1s spectrum (see Fig. 6(a)) the binding energy located at 284.3 eV corresponds to C‒C or C=C bonding. The binding energy appeared at 285.3 eV is assigned to the C‒N bond, whereas the binding energy shown at ca. 286.6 and 288.3 eV can be attributed to the C‒O and C=O bonds, respectively. Fig. 6(b) displays the N 1s spectrum that contains pyrrolic/pyridinic N (399.7 eV) and NOx (406.8 eV). As depicted in Fig. 6(c), the binding energy associated with the O1s spectrum can be realized at 529.9 eV (Zn‒O), 531.5 eV(C=O), and 532.7 eV (C‒OH). The Zn 2p3/2 peak (located at 1021.2 eV) and Zn 2p1/2 (1044.3 eV) are also provided in Fig. 6(d). The energy splitting between the Zn 2p3/2 and Zn 2p1/2 peaks demonstrates the presence of ZnO crystals within the composite structure.

To further analyze the bonding between the N-GQDs and ZnO crystals, the survey-scan and N 1s spectra of ZnO, N-GQD, and GQD/ZnO composite were obtained, as shown in Fig. S2(a). As tabulated in Table 1 , N content in the N-GQD sample is around 6.4 at.%. Fig. S2(b) shows high-resolution N 1s spectra of different samples. It is worth noting that pure ZnO sample does not include the C‒N bond (at ca. 399.68 eV), while the signal associated with the C‒N bond can be found for the GQD/ZnO composite. This finding indicates that the synthesis method developed in this study can successfully decorate the N-GQDs on the surface of ZnO matrix via strong chemical bonds.

Fig. 7 (a) and (c) demonstrates N2 adsorption/desorption isotherms of pure ZnO and GQD/ZnO composite. According to BET analysis, the specific surface areas of pure ZnO and GQD/ZnO composite were 15.0 m2/g and 24.8 m2/g, respectively. Fig. 7(b) and (d) show the pore size distribution of pure ZnO and GQD/ZnO composite, assessed by the BJH method. With the addition of N-GQDs, the average pore size of ZnO decreases from 18.2 to 6.2 nm. Indeed, through introduction of the N-GQDs into the mesoporous ZnO, nanosized N-GQDs will be embedded into the porous ZnO matrix, narrowing the pore size distribution of GQD/ZnO composite. Although the implementation of N-GQDs increases the specific surface area, its effect on the MNZ absorption equilibrium characteristic of GQD/ZnO composites seems to be insignificant, as depicted in Fig. S3. Thus, we speculate that the influence of specific surface area on the adsorption capacity as well as the photocatalytic degradation is insubstantial for the composite structures synthesized here.

Fig. S4 illustrates the FE-SEM images of pure ZnO and GQD/ZnO composites. According to Fig. S4, the ZnO matrix possesses a three-dimensional spherical structure, whereas the N-GQDs morphology cannot be viewed clearly within the GQD/ZnO composite. This is majorly due to the GQD particles covering the ZnO matrix. Therefore, it is very difficult to precisely explore the microstructural GQD/ZnO composites using FE-SEM microscopy. Accordingly, the HR-TEM was used to analyze the surface morphology of the samples.

Fig. 8 (a) and (b) demonstrate the HR-TEM micrograph and selected-area diffraction (SAD) pattern of the pure ZnO sample, respectively. As shown in Fig. 8, the ZnO matrix prepared by the co-precipitation method consists of needle-like crystals. The SAD pattern of the ZnO matrix includes single crystalline structure with well-defined crystalline planes (100), (002), (101), and (102) of wurtzite lattices. Considering Fig. 8(c) and (d), the GQDs, having an average particle size of ∼5 nm, are uniformly distributed with a relatively narrow particle size distribution. Fig. 8(e) shows HR-TEM micrograph of the GQD/ZnO composite, where GQDs are evenly dispersed onto the rod-shaped ZnO matrix. According to Fig. 8(f), the ZnO and N-GQDs are tightly bonded within the heterostructure. Indeed, the lattice spacing distances for the crystal plane of N-GQDs and the hexagonal ZnO structure were 3.32 Å and 2.42 Å, respectively. This trend indicates closely packed contact between the GQDs and ZnO lattice edges and confirms the formation of a heterostructure, which is consistent with the XPS analysis (see Fig. 6). Next, we used energy-dispersive X-ray (EDX) spectroscopy to analyze the surface mapping distribution and elemental content. As demonstrated in Fig. S5, three major elements, Zn, C, and O, are uniformly distributed on the GQD/ZnO composite. The EDX spectrum also confirms the presence of Zn, C, N, and O elements on the composite.

The adsorption saturation of MNZ molecules on the GQD/ZnO composite has been shown in Fig. S3. Upon reaching the adsorption equilibrium, further MNZ reduction can be attributed to the photocatalytic degradation induced by the composite photocatalysts. Thus, it is critical to confirm that the presence of the composite catalyst is necessary for proceeding the degradation reaction of MNZ when exposed to the UV-light. To this end, first, the degradation efficiency of MNZ by pure ZnO and GQD/ZnO-X composites (X = 0.3, 0.5, 1, 2, and 3) was investigated under the UV-light irradiation. To evaluate the photocatalytic performance, the removal ratio of MNZ, based on the ratio of the initial concentration (100 ppm) to the concentration after reaction time (0.5 h), was quantified and formulated as follows:
(1)

Fig. 9 (a) and (b) show the photocatalytic reaction kinetic curves and first-order reaction kinetic plots of pure ZnO and GQD/ZnO-X composites, respectively. As clearly illustrated in Fig. 9, the removal kinetic curve of pure ZnO becomes stable after ∼30 min under the UV irradiation, and its removal rate reaches as high as 96%. In contrast, the removal percentage of the GQD/ZnO-X (X = 0.3, 0.5, and 1) composites increases (to 98.3%, 99%, and 100%, respectively) after 0.5 h photocatalysis exposure. This observation indicates that an appropriate amount of N-GQDs effectively improves the photo-degradation kinetics and reaction rates.

According to Fig. 9(b), the first-order kinetic model very well describes the linear behavior of the experimental data (i.e., R-square: 0.965‒0.980). Therefore, the photocatalytic degradation of MNZ was analyzed by the first-order reaction kinetic model. Table 2 collects the reaction rate constant (k, in units of min−1), Removal (%), and R-square, quantified using this model. Among different composite samples, GQD/ZnO-1 catalyst exhibits highest k value (i.e., 0.157 min−1), which is 1.74 times higher than that of pure ZnO. Moreover, GQD/ZnO-1 catalyst also displays the best removal efficiency (∼100%) after 0.5 h UV irradiation. In contrast, the k value of GQD/ZnO-2 catalyst is very low (0.039 min−1) with a poor efficiency (∼72.5%). The undesired performance of GQD/ZnO-2 catalyst is presumably due to excessive GQDs loading on the ZnO matrix that significantly affects the light absorption and subsequent scattering of incident UV light. Accordingly, it does not optically activate the internal ZnO matrix, resulting in low surface coverage for the photo-degradation [35].

To provide more comprehensive comparison, commercially available TiO2 photocatalyst (P-25) was also employed for the photocatalytic degradation of MNZ, as shown in Fig. 10 . Based on Fig. 10(a), the P-25 catalyst has a removal rate of ∼95.3% after 30 min of reaction time, which is similar to that of pure ZnO (i.e., 96.0%). As depicted in Fig. 10(b), P-25 catalyst also demonstrates a first-order reaction kinetic behavior. The resulting rate constant for the TiO2 photocatalyst was 0.091 min−1, which is much lower than that of GQD/ZnO-1 catalyst. The finding indicates that as-synthesized GQD/ZnO composite serves as a high-performance photocatalyst for degrading MNZ in liquid phase under the UV-light irradiation.

To inspect the photocatalytic mechanism, cyclic voltammetry (CV) measurements were carried out to analyze the activity of different photoelectrodes. Fig. 11 (a) shows the CV profiles of pristine ZnO and GQD/ZnO-1 electrodes at 10 mV s−1. We observe that the photocurrent from GQD/ZnO electrode is three times higher than the pristine ZnO electrode. This reveals that the addition of N-GQDs effectively improves the photocurrent, thereby enhancing the effect of photocatalytic degradation of antibiotics. The improved photocatalytic activity can be explained by the schematic diagram of photo-induced electron dynamic pathway of GQD/ZnO composite under the UV irradiation, as illustrated in Fig. 11(b). In the binary catalyst, N-GQDs promote the absorption in visible-light region, increase the photo-induced charge carriers, and subsequently improve the photocatalytic activity [34,36]. Prior studies have also reported on the optical band gap of GQDs positioning within the range of 3.0‒3.5 eV, which is lower than that of the ZnO (∼4.2 eV) [38]. Within the structure of GQD/ZnO catalyst, the N-GQDs facilitate the photocurrent generation and prevent the electron-hole recombination from the pristine ZnO crystals under the UV irradiation. Thus, an optimal ratio of GQDs in ZnO matrix excessively boosts the photocatalytic activity and reaction kinetics toward MNZ degradation in liquid phase. Table S1 summarizes the comparison of literature reports of MNZ degradation. The as-synthesized GQD/ZnO exhibited superior performance compared to other materials. In addition, in order to confirm the stability and durability study of GQD/ZnO catalyst during photocatalytic process, we perform SEM (Fig. S6), XRD (Fig. S7) and XPS (Figs. S8 and S9) measurements of GQD/ZnO catalyst before and after photocatalytic reaction in terms of surface morphologies, crystal structure and charge distribution. All the results demonstrate that GQD/ZnO catalyst was very stable after photocatalytic process.

The photocatalytic mechanism of binary GQD/ZnO catalyst under the UV irradiation can be described through the following steps. When the GQD/ZnO composite is exposed to UV light, ZnO and GQDs are excited by the external and visible-light photons, respectively, resulting in the generation of electron-hole pairs. Meanwhile, the photo-induced electrons of GQDs are transferred from the conduction band (CB) of GQDs to CB of ZnO. The photo-generated holes move upward from the valence band (VB) of ZnO to VB of GQDs. This photo-induced process triggers the separation of the electron-hole pair at the interface between ZnO and GQDs within the heterostructure. Based on the UV-vis absorption spectrum, the light utilization from the ultraviolet to visible light is improved. On the N-functionalized GQDs, N-functionalities such as pyrrolic/pyridinic N and graphitic N can accelerate the charge transfer rate in the heterostructure. Accordingly, the functional groups on the GQD surface are advantageous for improving the photocatalytic activity. Analyzing the photocurrent profiles of as-synthesized composites, it is clear that the increased specific surface area for the adsorption of pollutants is not the major contributing constituent for vastly improved photodegradation rate. Thus, the unique design of heterostructures is the key factor for enhancing the degradation performance and boosting the light absorption efficiency of ZnO composite materials. Indeed, the N-GQDs facilitate the formation of the electron-hole pairs, which ultimately increases the charge carriers, and leads to an improved photodegradation efficiency.

In the study, we developed heterogeneous GQD/ZnO composite as a photocatalyst for degradation of the antibiotics in liquid phase. Through the thermal carbonization, N-functionalized GQDs, having an average particle size of ∼5 nm, were synthesized and well-dispersed within the ZnO composite structure. The co-precipitation method was employed to finely tune the ratio of GQD to ZnO in the binary GQD/ZnO catalysts. It was shown that the implementation of N-GQDs efficiently improves the photocatalytic activity as well as the reaction kinetic rate. Highly optimized GQD/ZnO composite exhibited ultra-high rate constant (∼1.74 times higher than the pristine ZnO). The improved photocatalytic activity was majorly attributed to the N-functionalized GQDs (e.g., pyrrolic/pyridinic N and graphitic N) accelerating the charge transfer in the heterostructure. Within the structure of GQD/ZnO catalyst, the N-GQDs plays a critical role in improving the photocurrent generation as well as preventing the electron-hole recombination from the pristine ZnO crystals exposed to the UV light. Therefore, the composite GQD/ZnO photocatalysts developed in this work can be employed for degrading MNZ in liquid phase under the UV-light irradiation with high efficiency and significantly improved kinetic reaction rate.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The authors are very grateful for the financial support the Ministry of Science and Technology of Taiwan (MOST 108–2221-E-155–036-MY3, 108EFD0500003, 108–2811-E-033–500, 109–2911-I-033–502, 108-E-033-MY3 and 109–3116-F-006–018). Partial financial support for this work under the grant number CMRPD2H0062 by the Chang Gung Memorial Hospital, Linkou, Taiwan (Chang Gung Medical Foundation, Taiwan) is deeply appreciated.
